payload.instructions:
## Skills
These skills are discovered at startup from multiple local sources. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions.
- skill-creator: Guide for creating effective skills. This skill should be used when users want to create a new skill (or update an existing skill) that extends Codex's capabilities with specialized knowledge, workflows, or tool integrations. (file: /Users/duhl/.codex/skills/.system/skill-creator/SKILL.md)
- skill-installer: Install Codex skills into $CODEX_HOME/skills from a curated list or a GitHub repo path. Use when a user asks to list installable skills, install a curated skill, or install a skill from another repo (including private repos). (file: /Users/duhl/.codex/skills/.system/skill-installer/SKILL.md)
- Discovery: Available skills are listed in project docs and may also appear in a runtime "## Skills" section (name + description + file path). These are the sources of truth; skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  3) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  4) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Description as trigger: The YAML `description` in `SKILL.md` is the primary trigger signal; rely on it to decide applicability. If unsure, ask a brief clarification before proceeding.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deeply nested references; prefer one-hop files explicitly linked from `SKILL.md`.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

content.text:
# AGENTS.md instructions for /Users/duhl/git/anedot-frontend

<INSTRUCTIONS>
## Skills
These skills are discovered at startup from multiple local sources. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions.
- skill-creator: Guide for creating effective skills. This skill should be used when users want to create a new skill (or update an existing skill) that extends Codex's capabilities with specialized knowledge, workflows, or tool integrations. (file: /Users/duhl/.codex/skills/.system/skill-creator/SKILL.md)
- skill-installer: Install Codex skills into $CODEX_HOME/skills from a curated list or a GitHub repo path. Use when a user asks to list installable skills, install a curated skill, or install a skill from another repo (including private repos). (file: /Users/duhl/.codex/skills/.system/skill-installer/SKILL.md)
- Discovery: Available skills are listed in project docs and may also appear in a runtime "## Skills" section (name + description + file path). These are the sources of truth; skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  3) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  4) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Description as trigger: The YAML `description` in `SKILL.md` is the primary trigger signal; rely on it to decide applicability. If unsure, ask a brief clarification before proceeding.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deeply nested references; prefer one-hop files explicitly linked from `SKILL.md`.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.
</INSTRUCTIONS>
what steps should I take to update this project from yarn to pnpm?

payload.message: what steps should I take to update this project from yarn to pnpm?

payload.output:
Exit code: 1
Wall time: 0.1 seconds
Output:

payload.message:
Here’s a pragmatic, low‑risk sequence you can follow to migrate from Yarn to pnpm:

**Prep**
- Ensure you’re on a clean branch; keep `yarn.lock` for rollback until the end.
- Confirm Node version alignment with `package.json` or `.nvmrc`.

**Switch tooling**
- Enable Corepack if you want managed pnpm: `corepack enable`.
- Add pnpm version pin (recommended) in `package.json`:
  - `packageManager`: `pnpm@X.Y.Z`

**Lockfile + install**
- Remove Yarn artifacts: `yarn.lock`, `.yarn/`, `.yarnrc.yml` (if present).
- Run `pnpm install` to generate `pnpm-lock.yaml`.

**Scripts + config**
- Update scripts that call `yarn` to `pnpm` (e.g. `yarn test` → `pnpm test`).
- Update CI workflows, Dockerfiles, and docs to use pnpm.
- If you used Yarn-specific features (resolutions, nohoist), map them:
  - `resolutions` → `pnpm.overrides`
  - `nohoist` → `pnpm.public-hoist-pattern` / `pnpm.shamefully-hoist`

**Workspace behavior**
- If this is a monorepo, ensure `pnpm-workspace.yaml` is present and correct.

**Cache/registry**
- If you rely on private registries, ensure `.npmrc` is correct for pnpm.

**Verify**
- Run the full test/build commands locally.
- Commit removal of Yarn files + addition of `pnpm-lock.yaml`.

If you want, I can scan the repo to spot Yarn-specific config and suggest exact file edits.

content.text:
Here’s a pragmatic, low‑risk sequence you can follow to migrate from Yarn to pnpm:

**Prep**
- Ensure you’re on a clean branch; keep `yarn.lock` for rollback until the end.
- Confirm Node version alignment with `package.json` or `.nvmrc`.

**Switch tooling**
- Enable Corepack if you want managed pnpm: `corepack enable`.
- Add pnpm version pin (recommended) in `package.json`:
  - `packageManager`: `pnpm@X.Y.Z`

**Lockfile + install**
- Remove Yarn artifacts: `yarn.lock`, `.yarn/`, `.yarnrc.yml` (if present).
- Run `pnpm install` to generate `pnpm-lock.yaml`.

**Scripts + config**
- Update scripts that call `yarn` to `pnpm` (e.g. `yarn test` → `pnpm test`).
- Update CI workflows, Dockerfiles, and docs to use pnpm.
- If you used Yarn-specific features (resolutions, nohoist), map them:
  - `resolutions` → `pnpm.overrides`
  - `nohoist` → `pnpm.public-hoist-pattern` / `pnpm.shamefully-hoist`

**Workspace behavior**
- If this is a monorepo, ensure `pnpm-workspace.yaml` is present and correct.

**Cache/registry**
- If you rely on private registries, ensure `.npmrc` is correct for pnpm.

**Verify**
- Run the full test/build commands locally.
- Commit removal of Yarn files + addition of `pnpm-lock.yaml`.

If you want, I can scan the repo to spot Yarn-specific config and suggest exact file edits.

143:export const credentialsMap: CredentialsMap = {
144:  accessToken: true,
145:  apiKey: true,
146:  appPassword: true,
147:  clientId: true,
148:  domain: true,
149:  email: true,
150:  listId: true,
151:  password: true,
152:  refreshToken: true,
153:  securityToken: true,
154:  url: true,
155:  username: true,
156:};
157:
158:export const integrationCredentialsKeys = Object.keys(
159:  credentialsMap
160:) as IntegrationCredentialsProperties[];
161:
162:const commonPropertiesMap = ({ t }: WithIntegrationTFunction) => {
163:  // can't set this as key prefix because re-using a general T function for integrationDirectory
164:  const prefix = 'form.validation';
165:
166:  return new Map<string, yup.AnyObject>([
167:    ['accessToken', yup.string().required(t(`${prefix}.accessToken`))],
168:    [
169:      'actionPageId',
170:      yup.string().when('$actionPages', {
171:        is: true,
172:        then: (s) => s.required(t(`${prefix}.actionPageId`)),
173:      }),
174:    ],
175:    ['apiKey', yup.string().required(t(`${prefix}.apiKey`))],
176:    [
177:      'campaignId',
178:      yup.string().when('$campaigns', {
179:        is: true,
180:        then: (s) => s.required(t(`${prefix}.campaignId`)),
181:      }),
182:    ],
183:    ['donationPagesOnly', yup.boolean()],
184:    ['email', yup.string().email()],
185:    ['excludedActionPageIds', yup.string()],
186:    ['excludedCampaignIds', yup.string()],
187:    ['listId', yup.string().required(t(`${prefix}.listId`))],
188:    ['listIds', yup.array(yup.string().nullable()).optional()],
189:    ['name', yup.string().required(t(`${prefix}.name`))],
190:    [
191:      'url',
192:      yup
193:        .string()
194:        .url()
195:        .required(t(`${prefix}.url`)),
196:    ],
197:    ['username', yup.string().required(t(`${prefix}.username`))],
198:    ['password', yup.string().required(t(`${prefix}.password`))],
199:    ['scopeId', yup.string()],
200:  ]);
201:};
202:
203:export type CommonPropertyDefaultKey = Exclude<
204:  IntegrationCommonProperties,
205:  'actionPageId' | 'campaignId'
206:>;
207:
208:export const commonPropertiesDefaults: Record<
209:  CommonPropertyDefaultKey,
210:  string | string[] | boolean
211:> = {
212:  accessToken: '',
213:  apiKey: '',
214:  appPassword: '',
215:  clientId: '',
216:  domain: '',
217:  donationPagesOnly: false,
218:  email: '',
219:  excludedActionPageIds: '',
220:  excludedCampaignIds: '',
221:  listId: '',
222:  listIds: [],
223:  name: '',
224:  password: '',
225:  refreshToken: '',
226:  scopeId: '',
227:  securityToken: '',
228:  url: '',
229:  username: '',
230:};
231:
232:export const composeIntegrationFormSchema = (
233:  keys: IntegrationCommonProperties[],
234:  options: {
235:    additionalProperties?: yup.ObjectShape;
236:  } & WithIntegrationTFunction
237:) => {
238:  const { additionalProperties, t } = options;
239:  const schema = keys.reduce(
240:    (obj, k) => ({
241:      ...obj,
242:      [k]: commonPropertiesMap({ t }).get(k),
243:    }),
244:    {}
245:  );
246:
247:  return yup.object({ ...schema, ...additionalProperties }) as yup.AnyObjectSchema;
248:};
